title: Otitis Media
oneLiner: Acute infection of the middle ear, common in children; most resolve spontaneously, but complications (mastoiditis, meningitis) need prompt recognition.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute mastoiditis** — post-auricular swelling/erythema, protruding pinna, fever; this is a surgical emergency → ENT referral + CT temporal bone
- **Intracranial complication (meningitis / sigmoid sinus thrombosis / brain abscess)** — signs of raised ICP (vomiting, bulging fontanelle, focal neurology, reduced GCS) or meningism (Kernig’s sign, photophobia); needs immediate LP (if safe) + IV antibiotics + urgent neuroimaging
- **Facial nerve palsy** — rare but indicates spread of infection to fallopian canal; needs urgent ENT + IV antibiotics ± mastoidectomy
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** ear pain (otalgia), fever, irritability in infants, hearing loss (conductive), recent upper respiratory tract infection (URTI) within preceding days
- **Key exam findings:** bulging, erythematous, and/or immobile tympanic membrane (TM) on pneumatic otoscopy; otorrhoea (if TM perforated); conductive hearing loss (Rinne’s test: bone > air)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Acute otitis externa** — pain on pinna manipulation, ear canal oedema/debris, no TM bulging (can see normal TM if canal patent)
- **Otitis media with effusion (glue ear)** — no pain, no fever; dull TM with fluid level (not bulging); normal mobility on pneumatic otoscopy
- **Foreign body / cerumen impaction** — no fever, normal TM beyond obstruction
- **Temporomandibular joint (TMJ) pain** — no otoscopic findings, pain on jaw movement
- **Acute mastoiditis** — post-auricular swelling, pinna displacement, ± TM changes; if present, treat as above
- **Splitter:** **Pinna tenderness/mobility pain** → otitis externa (if present) vs. **TM bulging** → otitis media
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Pneumatic otoscopy (key): assess TM mobility, colour, position (bulging/retracted), presence of perforation or effusion
- **Bloods:** Not routine; FBC + CRP if febrile, systemically unwell, or suspected complications
- **Imaging:** Not routine for uncomplicated AOM; CT temporal bone (with contrast) if suspected mastoiditis or intracranial extension; MRI head/venogram if sinus thrombosis suspected
- **Special tests:** Tympanometry (audiology clinic, not acute); hearing test (audiogram) after resolution if concerns
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** Use NICE definition: acute onset (<48h) of otalgia ± fever, with bulging TM or recent otorrhoea; no need for formal score
- **Never-miss test:** **Pneumatic otoscopy** — to confirm middle ear inflammation; omit = misdiagnosis of external canal pathology
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If systemically well, no red flags → analgesia (paracetamol ± ibuprofen) + deferred antibiotics (watchful waiting)
  - If high fever, severe pain, bilateral AOM in <2yr, or immunocompromised → prescribe antibiotics (amoxicillin first line)
  - If mastoiditis / intracranial complication → IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav or ceftriaxone) + urgent ENT referral
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Not normally admitted unless complications (mastoiditis, meningitis); if admitted:
  - IV antibiotics (as per local policy; typically co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS or ceftriaxone 2g IV od) ± metronidazole if intracranial involvement
  - Analgesia as needed; ENT review for surgical intervention (myringotomy, mastoidectomy)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If antibiotics given: amoxicillin 7 days (children: 30-50mg/kg TDS, max 500mg TDS) OR if allergic: erythromycin 7 days (child: 30-50mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses) OR clarithromycin 7 days (child: 15mg/kg BD)
  - Watchful waiting: advise to return if no improvement in 48-72h or worsening
  - No routine follow‑up; if recurrent AOM (≥3 episodes in 6 months or ≥4 in 12 months) → refer ENT for possible grommets
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** IV fluids (0.9% NaCl + 5% dextrose if child <5yr, check local policy) at maintenance rate; VTE prophylaxis (if adult and reduced mobility); diet as tolerated; observations: NEWS2 4‑hourly (more frequent if unwell)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Amoxicillin (child 30-50mg/kg TDS PO, max 500mg TDS; adult 500mg TDS PO) for 7 days
  - Erythromycin (if penicillin allergic; child 30-50mg/kg/day PO in 4 divided doses; adult 250-500mg QDS) for 7 days
  - Clarithromycin (alternative; child 15mg/kg BD PO, adult 250-500mg BD) for 7 days
- **Common alternatives:** Co-amoxiclav (if initial amoxicillin failure, but not first line due to resistance risk); ceftriaxone IV for severe infection
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Amoxicillin – avoid if history of immediate hypersensitivity (anaphylaxis); erythromycin – risk of QT prolongation, interacts with warfarin, statins, and many others; clarithromycin similar interactions plus contraindicated with colchicine
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment needed for short course; for severe renal impairment, extend dosing interval for penicillin/cephalosporins (check BNF)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Overprescribing antibiotics** – NICE recommends “no antibiotic” or “delayed prescription” for most cases; giving unnecessary antibiotics drives resistance; in kids, avoid high‑dose amoxicillin (≥90mg/kg/day) unless confirmed severe pneumococcal infection
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** TM perforation (usually self‑heals with otorrhoea – often relieves pain); acute mastoiditis (see above); facial nerve palsy (rare); labyrinthitis (vertigo, nausea, nystagmus)
- **Chronic:** Chronic suppurative otitis media (persistent otorrhoea >6 weeks ± perforation); cholesteatoma (requires mastoid surgery); adhesive otitis media (atelectasis); permanent conductive hearing loss; untreated intracranial spread (brain abscess, sigmoid sinus thrombosis, meningitis – high mortality)
sections.Prognosis: - Around 80% of uncomplicated AOM resolve spontaneously within 2‑3 days; antibiotics shorten symptom duration by about 1 day; recurrence common in young children (20-30% in first year). Risk of complications low (<1%). With mastoiditis, mortality still 2‑3% despite modern antibiotics.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always document pneumatic otoscopy findings** – bulging TM is the most specific sign; do not rely on red TM alone (can be normal crying child)
- **Use watchful waiting with a “safety‑net” prescription** – offer delayed antibiotic (to collect after 48h if no improvement) to reduce unnecessary use
- **In children <2yr with bilateral AOM** – NICE says immediate antibiotics (higher complication risk); otherwise offer no/delayed prescription
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT give antibiotics immediately for all cases** – most are viral or self‑limiting; overuse contributes to resistance and side effects (diarrhoea, rash)
- **Do NOT prescribe fluoroquinolone ear drops** for acute otitis media (they are for otitis externa); they do not reach the middle ear through an intact TM
- **Do NOT ignore persistent post‑auricular swelling** – assume mastoiditis until proven otherwise; do not treat with oral antibiotics alone
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Fever >72h, increasing pain, new post‑auricular swelling/redness, hearing loss not resolving after 1 week, vomiting or reduced consciousness (possible intracranial spread), or if child becomes lethargic/irritable
- **Follow-up:** Usually not needed; if TM perforation with otorrhoea for >6 weeks → ENT clinic; if recurrent infections → ENT for consideration of grommets
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ENT on call if:** Suspected acute mastoiditis (post‑auricular swelling + fever + TM changes), facial nerve palsy, or any signs of meningitis (meningism, photophobia, altered GCS) or raised ICP (bradycardia, hypertension, bulging fontanelle)
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥4 (adult) or PEWS ≥3 (child) with SIRS criteria; GCS ≤14; lactate >2 mmol/L; CRP >100 mg/L with fever and ear findings

rawMarkdown:
## Otitis Media
**One-liner:** Acute infection of the middle ear, common in children; most resolve spontaneously, but complications (mastoiditis, meningitis) need prompt recognition.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute mastoiditis** — post-auricular swelling/erythema, protruding pinna, fever; this is a surgical emergency → ENT referral + CT temporal bone
- **Intracranial complication (meningitis / sigmoid sinus thrombosis / brain abscess)** — signs of raised ICP (vomiting, bulging fontanelle, focal neurology, reduced GCS) or meningism (Kernig’s sign, photophobia); needs immediate LP (if safe) + IV antibiotics + urgent neuroimaging
- **Facial nerve palsy** — rare but indicates spread of infection to fallopian canal; needs urgent ENT + IV antibiotics ± mastoidectomy

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** ear pain (otalgia), fever, irritability in infants, hearing loss (conductive), recent upper respiratory tract infection (URTI) within preceding days
- **Key exam findings:** bulging, erythematous, and/or immobile tympanic membrane (TM) on pneumatic otoscopy; otorrhoea (if TM perforated); conductive hearing loss (Rinne’s test: bone > air)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Acute otitis externa** — pain on pinna manipulation, ear canal oedema/debris, no TM bulging (can see normal TM if canal patent)
- **Otitis media with effusion (glue ear)** — no pain, no fever; dull TM with fluid level (not bulging); normal mobility on pneumatic otoscopy
- **Foreign body / cerumen impaction** — no fever, normal TM beyond obstruction
- **Temporomandibular joint (TMJ) pain** — no otoscopic findings, pain on jaw movement
- **Acute mastoiditis** — post-auricular swelling, pinna displacement, ± TM changes; if present, treat as above
- **Splitter:** **Pinna tenderness/mobility pain** → otitis externa (if present) vs. **TM bulging** → otitis media

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Pneumatic otoscopy (key): assess TM mobility, colour, position (bulging/retracted), presence of perforation or effusion
- **Bloods:** Not routine; FBC + CRP if febrile, systemically unwell, or suspected complications
- **Imaging:** Not routine for uncomplicated AOM; CT temporal bone (with contrast) if suspected mastoiditis or intracranial extension; MRI head/venogram if sinus thrombosis suspected
- **Special tests:** Tympanometry (audiology clinic, not acute); hearing test (audiogram) after resolution if concerns
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** Use NICE definition: acute onset (<48h) of otalgia ± fever, with bulging TM or recent otorrhoea; no need for formal score
- **Never-miss test:** **Pneumatic otoscopy** — to confirm middle ear inflammation; omit = misdiagnosis of external canal pathology

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If systemically well, no red flags → analgesia (paracetamol ± ibuprofen) + deferred antibiotics (watchful waiting)
  - If high fever, severe pain, bilateral AOM in <2yr, or immunocompromised → prescribe antibiotics (amoxicillin first line)
  - If mastoiditis / intracranial complication → IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav or ceftriaxone) + urgent ENT referral
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Not normally admitted unless complications (mastoiditis, meningitis); if admitted:
  - IV antibiotics (as per local policy; typically co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS or ceftriaxone 2g IV od) ± metronidazole if intracranial involvement
  - Analgesia as needed; ENT review for surgical intervention (myringotomy, mastoidectomy)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If antibiotics given: amoxicillin 7 days (children: 30-50mg/kg TDS, max 500mg TDS) OR if allergic: erythromycin 7 days (child: 30-50mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses) OR clarithromycin 7 days (child: 15mg/kg BD)
  - Watchful waiting: advise to return if no improvement in 48-72h or worsening
  - No routine follow‑up; if recurrent AOM (≥3 episodes in 6 months or ≥4 in 12 months) → refer ENT for possible grommets
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** IV fluids (0.9% NaCl + 5% dextrose if child <5yr, check local policy) at maintenance rate; VTE prophylaxis (if adult and reduced mobility); diet as tolerated; observations: NEWS2 4‑hourly (more frequent if unwell)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Amoxicillin (child 30-50mg/kg TDS PO, max 500mg TDS; adult 500mg TDS PO) for 7 days
  - Erythromycin (if penicillin allergic; child 30-50mg/kg/day PO in 4 divided doses; adult 250-500mg QDS) for 7 days
  - Clarithromycin (alternative; child 15mg/kg BD PO, adult 250-500mg BD) for 7 days
- **Common alternatives:** Co-amoxiclav (if initial amoxicillin failure, but not first line due to resistance risk); ceftriaxone IV for severe infection
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Amoxicillin – avoid if history of immediate hypersensitivity (anaphylaxis); erythromycin – risk of QT prolongation, interacts with warfarin, statins, and many others; clarithromycin similar interactions plus contraindicated with colchicine
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment needed for short course; for severe renal impairment, extend dosing interval for penicillin/cephalosporins (check BNF)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Overprescribing antibiotics** – NICE recommends “no antibiotic” or “delayed prescription” for most cases; giving unnecessary antibiotics drives resistance; in kids, avoid high‑dose amoxicillin (≥90mg/kg/day) unless confirmed severe pneumococcal infection

### Complications
- **Acute:** TM perforation (usually self‑heals with otorrhoea – often relieves pain); acute mastoiditis (see above); facial nerve palsy (rare); labyrinthitis (vertigo, nausea, nystagmus)
- **Chronic:** Chronic suppurative otitis media (persistent otorrhoea >6 weeks ± perforation); cholesteatoma (requires mastoid surgery); adhesive otitis media (atelectasis); permanent conductive hearing loss; untreated intracranial spread (brain abscess, sigmoid sinus thrombosis, meningitis – high mortality)

### Prognosis
- Around 80% of uncomplicated AOM resolve spontaneously within 2‑3 days; antibiotics shorten symptom duration by about 1 day; recurrence common in young children (20-30% in first year). Risk of complications low (<1%). With mastoiditis, mortality still 2‑3% despite modern antibiotics.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always document pneumatic otoscopy findings** – bulging TM is the most specific sign; do not rely on red TM alone (can be normal crying child)
- **Use watchful waiting with a “safety‑net” prescription** – offer delayed antibiotic (to collect after 48h if no improvement) to reduce unnecessary use
- **In children <2yr with bilateral AOM** – NICE says immediate antibiotics (higher complication risk); otherwise offer no/delayed prescription

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT give antibiotics immediately for all cases** – most are viral or self‑limiting; overuse contributes to resistance and side effects (diarrhoea, rash)
- **Do NOT prescribe fluoroquinolone ear drops** for acute otitis media (they are for otitis externa); they do not reach the middle ear through an intact TM
- **Do NOT ignore persistent post‑auricular swelling** – assume mastoiditis until proven otherwise; do not treat with oral antibiotics alone

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Fever >72h, increasing pain, new post‑auricular swelling/redness, hearing loss not resolving after 1 week, vomiting or reduced consciousness (possible intracranial spread), or if child becomes lethargic/irritable
- **Follow-up:** Usually not needed; if TM perforation with otorrhoea for >6 weeks → ENT clinic; if recurrent infections → ENT for consideration of grommets

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ENT on call if:** Suspected acute mastoiditis (post‑auricular swelling + fever + TM changes), facial nerve palsy, or any signs of meningitis (meningism, photophobia, altered GCS) or raised ICP (bradycardia, hypertension, bulging fontanelle)
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥4 (adult) or PEWS ≥3 (child) with SIRS criteria; GCS ≤14; lactate >2 mmol/L; CRP >100 mg/L with fever and ear findings
